metadata.title: Research the Dangerous Middle: What the Bias Literature Actually Says

beats.narration_text: LLM grading accuracy jumped from 55% to 93% between 2022 and 2025. Most teachers think that means it's now safe to delegate. The research says the opposite.

The improvement makes the problem worse, not better. At 90% correlation, the 10% disagreement is exactly where teacher judgment matters most — and it concentrates in predictable categories: ESL students, AAVE writers, non-standard naming conventions. As AI grading accuracy improves, the remaining disagreements become more systematically biased, not less.

Synthesize the 2024 to 2026 bias literature: ESL false positive rates, AAVE underperformance, contrastive-learning mitigation and its limits, the Anthropic Education Report 2025 finding. Flag any figures that can't be verified.

props.command: claude "Research and synthesize the 2024-2026 literature on AI grading bias: (1) ESL false positive rates in AI detection tools, (2) AAVE underperformance in NLP grading systems, (3) the contrastive-learning mitigation approach from arXiv:2601.16724 and its limits, (4) the Anthropic Education Report 2025 finding on automation-heavy grading. Produce a 700-word sourced brief and a 3-row summary table: Study | Key Finding | Implication for Classroom Use. Flag any figures you cannot verify."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. Three-row summary table: Study, Key Finding, Implication. UNVERIFIED markers on any figure Claude cannot confirm.

props.code:
| Study | Key Finding | Implication |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| Stanford 2024 ESL | ESL students flagged as
AI-authored at elevated rates
[UNVERIFIED: exact %) | Human review required
for all ESL submissions |
| AI Ethics Inst. 2024 | AAVE features penalized
by NLP rubric scorers | Manual rubric calibration
for dialectal variation |
| Anthropic Ed. Report 2025 | 48.9% automation-heavy
use correlated with lower
outcomes | Teacher-in-the-loop
required at evaluation stage |

**Mitigation note:** Contrastive-learning approach
(arXiv:2601.16724) shows ~40% reduction in bias
— not elimination. Human review remains required.

beats.narration_text: Brief generated: 694 words, sourced. Claude flags 2 specific statistics it cannot verify with confidence — these appear with UNVERIFIED markers. The mitigation finding is correctly stated: 40% reduction in bias, not elimination. Both UNVERIFIED markers confirmed in chapter sources.
Ask Claude to produce a one-page plain-language version for parents and administrators.

props.command: claude "Convert the bias brief to a one-page plain-language version for parents and administrators. No jargon. Preserve the key numbers. Show what precision is lost in the translation."

beats.narration_text: Plain-language version: 380 words, no jargon. What gets lost in translation: the arXiv citation is paraphrased as 'recent research,' the 40% reduction figure is preserved, the narrowing principle is simplified. The comparison shows what the teacher must add back when sharing with a technical versus a policy audience.

The narrowing principle is what makes the dangerous middle genuinely dangerous: the 10% disagreement is not random noise — it is systematic, predictable, and concentrated in the student populations that most need accurate feedback. The human's job is to audit the 10% that AI gets systematically wrong, which requires knowing which student populations are in that 10%.
That's the full Codex for Teachers series — ten tools for conducting AI responsibly in the classroom.
Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. At NikBearBrown, nikbearbrown dot com.
